question: Which stage of memory brings stored information back into conscious awareness?
related_info: Retrieval is the stage in which stored memories are brought back into conscious awareness so they can be used.
why_high_yield: High yield because the memory lecture is organized around the four-stage memory process, making the sequence itself exam-worthy.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (all Module 2 subjects included)
Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 2-7

question: Transforming a temporary, fragile memory into a more stable and long-lasting form is called ______.
related_info: Consolidation is the process by which a labile memory becomes more stable over time.
why_high_yield: High yield because consolidation is one of the key named memory stages and connects directly with the sleep lecture from Module 1.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 3-5

question: Why does unattended sensory information usually disappear quickly?

options.A: Because it is stored permanently in long-term memory.
options.B: Because the sensory register holds it only briefly unless attention selects it.
options.C: Because retrieval cues immediately erase it.
options.D: Because rehearsal makes it less accessible.

related_info: The lecture explains that sensory memory retains information accurately but only very briefly, and unattended input fades rapidly.
why_high_yield: High yield because forgetting begins early in the memory system, and the lecture uses this point to explain why attention matters for learning.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 9-11

question: Which study strategy best reflects elaborative rehearsal?

options.A: Repeating a definition aloud without connecting it to anything else
options.B: Linking a new concept to prior knowledge and organizing it meaningfully
options.C: Ignoring examples in order to focus on memorization speed
options.D: Reading notes once right before sleeping

related_info: Elaborative rehearsal improves long-term memory by connecting new information to existing knowledge and organizing it meaningfully.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture contrasts shallow repetition with deep processing, a very common applied-memory exam theme.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 11-18

question: Breaking a long string of information into smaller, meaningful units is called ______.
related_info: Chunking helps memory by organizing large amounts of information into smaller meaningful units, making them easier to encode and hold.
why_high_yield: High yield because chunking is one of the lecture's clearest memory-improvement techniques and is easy to test with examples.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 13-15

question: If the police ask you to describe a suspect from memory, they are testing ______; if they ask you to pick the suspect from a lineup, they are testing ______.
related_info: Recall requires generating information without the answer present, whereas recognition is easier because the correct answer is among the options and retrieval cues are available.
why_high_yield: High yield because recall versus recognition appears directly in the Exam 1 sample multiple-choice questions.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (sample question on recall vs recognition)
Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 19-20

question: When older information disrupts your ability to learn or remember newer information, the problem is called ______.
related_info: Proactive interference occurs when older material disrupts retrieval or learning of newer material; retroactive interference works in the opposite direction.
why_high_yield: High yield because proactive-versus-retroactive interference is a classic contrast question in introductory memory units.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 19-22

question: Which practice schedule is most likely to produce the spacing effect?

options.A: Studying the same material in one long cram session
options.B: Spreading several shorter study sessions across time
options.C: Reading without ever trying to recall

related_info: The spacing effect means that practice distributed over time is more effective than the same amount of practice massed closely together.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly labels the spacing effect as a practical memory-improvement strategy for studying.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 23-24

question: Which study behavior best reflects retrieval practice?

options.A: Rereading the same notes again and again without testing yourself
options.B: Setting the list aside and trying to recall it from memory
options.C: Sleeping less so you can read longer
options.D: Highlighting every sentence in the textbook

related_info: Retrieval practice strengthens memory by making learners actively bring information back to mind instead of only re-exposing themselves to it.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture presents retrieval practice as one of the most actionable study strategies, making it a natural testable application.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, p. 24

question: Participants who report seeing broken glass after hearing a more dramatic question about a car accident illustrate the ______.
related_info: The misinformation effect shows that memory is reconstructive and can be altered by suggestive post-event information.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses the Loftus and Palmer example to show that memory is not a literal video recording.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do you forget things you have studied.pdf, pp. 25-28

question: Which description best fits System 1 thinking?
related_info: The lecture presents System 1 as rapid and intuitive, whereas System 2 is slower, more effortful, and rule-based.
why_high_yield: High yield because Kahneman's two-systems framework organizes the whole decision-making lecture.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 2-4
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Bounded rationality means that judgments and decisions are limited by ______.

options.B: psychological and contextual constraints such as attention, information, and time pressure
options.C: the inability to learn from experience

related_info: The lecture explains that even capable thinkers are bounded by limited information, attentional resources, motivation, persistence, time pressure, and other contextual factors.
why_high_yield: High yield because bounded rationality is the core answer to the module's title question about sticking with bad investments.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 2-4
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Believing that a lucky charm can influence the outcome of a chance event is an example of ______.
related_info: Magical thinking is the belief that an object, action, or circumstance not logically related to an event can influence its outcome.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is a memorable illustration of illogical beliefs undermining rational judgment, which is a repeated theme in the lecture.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 5-6

question: Preferring a lottery ticket that you personally chose over an identical ticket given to you by someone else best reflects the ______.
related_info: The illusion of control is the tendency to believe one can influence outcomes that are actually uncontrollable, such as lottery results.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture gives direct lottery-ticket examples, making this concept especially easy to convert into exam scenarios.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 7-12
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: When people choose the sure option in a gain frame but the risky option in a loss frame, they are showing the ______.
related_info: The framing effect occurs when judgment changes depending on how objectively equivalent information is presented, such as lives saved versus lives lost.
why_high_yield: High yield because framing is one of the lecture's main heuristics-and-biases examples and is a standard introductory exam topic.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 15-17
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Changing the default setting on printers from single-sided to double-sided printing without removing any option is an example of a ______.
related_info: A nudge changes choice architecture so that decisions shift without changing the available options themselves.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture gives the printer-default study as a concrete applied example of behavioral decision making.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 17-19
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Eating more food simply because it was served in a larger bowl or on a larger plate best illustrates ______.
related_info: Mindless eating refers to the strong influence of seemingly irrelevant environmental factors, such as container or plate size, on eating behavior.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses several intuitive eating examples to show how context shapes decisions outside conscious analytic reasoning.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 20-24

question: After black appears on the roulette wheel five times in a row, betting on red because it 'must be next' reflects the ______.
related_info: The gambler's fallacy is the mistaken belief that a chance event becomes more likely because it has not occurred recently or because the opposite outcome has repeated.
why_high_yield: High yield because this is one of the clearest heuristics-and-biases examples in the decision-making lecture.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 25-28
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Judging 'bank teller and feminist' as more likely than 'bank teller' alone is a classic example of the ______.
related_info: The conjunction fallacy occurs when people incorrectly judge the probability of two events occurring together as greater than the probability of either event alone.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture links the Linda problem directly to representativeness, making it a classic exam-ready example.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 29-31
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Worrying about plane crashes immediately after hearing about several recent crashes in the news is the best example of ______.
related_info: Availability bias occurs when people judge the probability of an event by how easily examples come to mind rather than by complete or representative evidence.
why_high_yield: High yield because availability bias appears directly in the Exam 1 sample multiple-choice questions.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (sample question on availability bias)
Module 2/Lecture Slides - why do people stick to bad investments.pdf, pp. 32-35
Bazerman, M. H. (2023). Judgment and decision making (required reading)

question: Fluid intelligence refers most directly to the ability to ______.

options.A: use previously learned vocabulary and general knowledge
options.B: think and reason without relying on learned knowledge

related_info: The lecture contrasts fluid intelligence, which supports novel reasoning, with crystallized intelligence, which draws on accumulated knowledge.
why_high_yield: High yield because fluid-versus-crystallized intelligence is the opening conceptual contrast in the intelligence lecture.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 2-3

question: Norming in intelligence testing means ______.

options.A: allowing each test taker to choose their own scoring system
options.B: collecting data from a representative sample so scores can be interpreted relative to a population
options.C: making every test item equally difficult
options.D: removing all cultural influences from testing

related_info: Norming provides comparison data from a representative sample so that a person's score can be interpreted relative to peers.
why_high_yield: High yield because standardization and norming are key definitions in the history and logic of IQ testing.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 4-8

question: Spearman's g-factor refers to ______.

options.A: the tendency for people to show a growth mindset
options.B: a single hypothetical factor underlying many intellectual abilities
options.C: a measure of emotional intelligence only
options.D: the average IQ score of a group

related_info: Spearman proposed that performance across many mental tasks is positively correlated because of a general underlying factor, g.
why_high_yield: High yield because g is the lecture's main theoretical answer to why diverse cognitive abilities often correlate.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 5-7

question: Which research designs are especially useful for estimating heritability?

options.A: Gestalt demonstrations and psychophysical threshold studies

related_info: The lecture explains heritability using adoption studies that compare biological versus adoptive resemblance and twin studies that compare identical and fraternal twins.
why_high_yield: High yield because heritability is a core interpretive topic in the intelligence lecture and depends on recognizing these study designs.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 9-10

question: According to the lecture, an additional year of education is associated with roughly ______.

options.A: no change in IQ at all

related_info: The lecture cites a large meta-analysis suggesting that an additional year of education is associated with about a 1-to-5-point IQ increase.
why_high_yield: High yield because it shows that IQ is influenced by environment rather than being fixed, which directly answers the module's guiding question.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 10-11

question: When making a negative group stereotype salient worsens test performance for members of that group, the effect is called ______.
related_info: Stereotype threat refers to anxiety or arousal triggered by a negative stereotype, which can depress performance on the very task being evaluated.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses stereotype threat to show that group differences in test scores are not fixed and can depend on context.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 11-12

question: Worldwide gains in IQ scores across decades are known as the ______.
related_info: The Flynn effect refers to large long-term increases in measured IQ scores, especially on tasks involving abstract reasoning.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is a named effect that strongly supports the lecture's argument that intelligence test performance is shaped by historical and environmental conditions.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 13-14

question: Why can highly intelligent people still make irrational decisions and judgments?

options.A: Because IQ automatically causes stronger emotions than cognition.
options.B: Because IQ is unrelated to biases such as myside bias and does not guarantee rational thinking.
options.C: Because high IQ people cannot use System 2 thinking.
options.D: Because intelligence tests only measure sleep quality.

related_info: The lecture explicitly states that high IQ does not guarantee rational thinking and notes that IQ is unrelated to myside bias.
why_high_yield: High yield because this exact issue appears as the sample short-answer question in the Exam 1 Guidelines.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (sample short-answer question on high IQ and irrational judgment)
Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 17-18

question: In the lecture, myside bias refers to ______.

options.A: judging evidence in a way biased toward one's prior beliefs and opinions
options.C: the tendency to overestimate rare dramatic events because of news coverage
options.D: group members coordinating poorly on joint tasks

related_info: Myside bias is the tendency to evaluate and generate evidence in ways that support one's existing beliefs, regardless of intelligence level.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses myside bias to answer why IQ alone cannot guarantee good judgment.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 17-18

question: According to Sternberg's triarchic theory, practical intelligence is best described as ______.

options.A: the ability to solve only abstract verbal analogies
options.B: tacit, experience-based know-how for real-world problems
options.D: the tendency to prefer familiar options

related_info: The lecture defines practical intelligence as context-specific, experience-based know-how for solving ill-defined real-world problems.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture closes by moving beyond IQ, so practical intelligence is central to the answer that success depends on more than test scores.
source_refs: Module 2/Lecture Slides - does a high IQ guarantee success.pdf, pp. 19-23
